cards.title: Copy supports the creative
cards.front: If you had to rank copy vs. video, which carries the ad?
cards.back: Ad copy plays a SUPPORTING role to a great video. The 13 Elements of Persuasive Ad Copy are 'seasonings' that reinforce hooks and offers - they don't replace a strong creative. A solid selling video needs LESS copy because the video does the selling.

cards.title: Write for Homer, not Hawking
cards.front: What reading level should your ad copy target to stop the thumb scroll?
cards.back: Write at ~5th-grade level - clear, concise, cut-to-the-chase. 'Write for Homer (Simpson), not Hawking.' Justification: 50% of US adults can't read at an 8th-grade level, and Trump spoke at ~5th-grade level in 2016. Big words hurt conversions and scale.

cards.title: Simplify every word
cards.front: One word or two? What's the rule for word choice in ad copy?
cards.back: SIMPLIFY: use one word instead of two or three, prefer one-syllable words, and write at a low reading level. Big words hurt conversions and scale. This is Element 2 of the 13 Elements of Persuasive Ad Copy.

cards.title: Write in your own voice
cards.front: Why is copying a competitor's winning ad word-for-word a losing move?
cards.back: BE AUTHENTIC (Element 1): write in your own voice, don't copy verbatim. Authentic AND effective beats merely effective. Call out the avatar and a pain point (Policy-compliant). Copying others' copy is quick but ineffective - authenticity wins.

cards.title: The 'You' Rules
cards.front: Why does second-person 'you' grab attention - and when does it become a Policy violation?
cards.back:
'You' creates possession/ownership and captures attention; default to it (Element 8). But:
- NEVER tie 'you' to a personal attribute (Policy violation)
- Don't overuse it (creepy)
Compliant: 'superfoods are some of the highest vibing food you will ever come across.'
cards.example: Violation: 'Do you want to lose weight?' / 'Do you have diabetes?'

cards.title: Always use the CTA button
cards.front: URL in the copy is enough, right? What does the book insist on?
cards.back: Always use the Call-to-Action button on the ad, not just a URL. Make the next action crystal clear because people are skimming.

cards.title: Ask Questions, Answer Immediately
cards.front: You ask a question in your ad. What must come on the very next line?
cards.back: ASK QUESTIONS (Element 5) then ANSWER IMMEDIATELY (Element 6): give the answer on the next line, or tell them exactly what to do next. People want to be led. 'Show people you can help them by actually helping them.'

cards.title: Use Revealing Words (not hyperbole)
cards.front: Words like 'Discover' and 'hidden' pull readers in - so why not pile on the adjectives?
cards.back: USE REVEALING WORDS (Element 7): words implying discovery/novelty (Discover, hidden, astonishing) - but NOT hyperbole. Keep adjectives minimal: action verbs over adjectives at >= 2:1 (eyeball it, don't literally count). Avoid superlatives (best, finest, outstanding).

cards.title: Callouts, Capitals & Ellipses
cards.front: Three formatting tricks that frame your key idea - and the one that looks scammy if overdone?
cards.back:
- NON-STANDARD CALLOUTS (Element 9): frame novel points with brackets/parentheses, e.g. [Special Discount], [Don't miss your opportunity].
- CAPITALIZE (Element 10): capitalize ONLY the one word/phrase to remember (e.g. 'FREE TRAINING'). Never all-caps everything (scammy; Policy notices).
- EMBRACE THE ELLIPSIS (Element 11): a broken thought needing resolution on the next line, underscoring scarcity.
cards.example: 'click here to get your FREE copy . . . while they last'

cards.title: Employ Emojis
cards.front: What did split tests reveal about adding emojis to ad copy?
cards.back: EMPLOY EMOJIS (Element 13): punctuate benefits and the main message; don't overdo it. Split-test results: higher CTR, higher Relevance Score, lower CPC, lower CPA.

cards.title: The 13 Elements of Persuasive Ad Copy
cards.front: Which named framework gives you 13 copywriting 'seasonings' to spice up an ad?
cards.back:
The 13 Elements of Persuasive Ad Copy (Ralph Burns / Dominate Web Media). Use sparingly - you do NOT need all in every ad:
1. Be Authentic
2. Simplify
3. Write for Homer
4. Show Proof
5. Ask Questions
6. Answer Immediately
7. Use Revealing Words
8. 'You' Rules
9. Non-Standard Callouts
10. Capitalize
11. Embrace the Ellipsis
12. Mix It Up
13. Employ Emojis
(Curiosity was the original 14th element, now in Ch. 18.)

cards.title: The 7 Ad Building Blocks
cards.front: What are the Lego-style components you mix-and-match to build winning Facebook ads?
cards.back:
The 7 Ad Building Blocks (Facebook Ad Builder, Ch. 18). Mix-and-match to build ads, generate test variations, and reduce ad fatigue. Not every ad needs all seven; any order works:
1. Curiosity
2. Challenge / Frustration / Pain or Desire
3. Benefits and Desires
4. Credibility or Authority Statement
5. Give an 'Aha Moment'
6. Call-to-Action (CTA)
7. Overcoming Objections (mini-FAQ)
How to use: brainstorm many options per block first to build a deep inventory to assemble and test quickly.

cards.title: Surface the deep desire with 'so you can'
cards.front: How do you turn a flat product benefit into the deep desire behind it?
cards.back: Append 'so you can' / 'so that' to any benefit to surface the deep desire (Building Block #3). Speak to the conversation in the prospect's mind, not the product features. Watch out: benefit claims can trip Facebook Ad Policy - read the policy carefully.
cards.example: Samcart sells checkout software but the ad sells 'doubling your sales.'

cards.title: Credibility / Authority Statement
cards.front: Why should people listen to you - and can borrowed credibility count?
cards.back: Building Block #4: state why people should listen to, trust, and obey you. Inherited/borrowed credibility counts (e.g. airline experience reused for a checklist metaphor). Avoid sounding boastful.

cards.title: Show Proof to cold audiences
cards.front: Cold audience won't trust you - where do you put your credibility statements?
cards.back: SHOW PROOF (Element 4): for cold audiences, insert credibility statements THROUGHOUT, each tied to a pain point (usually longer copy). Make the incredible credible.

cards.title: Mini-FAQ after the first CTA
cards.front: Where can adding a mini-FAQ lift conversions, trust AND relevance score?
cards.back: Building Block #7 (Overcoming Objections): place a mini-FAQ AFTER the first CTA. It increases conversions, trust, and relevance scores even when it lengthens the ad. 'It flat out works.'

cards.title: Soft CTA timing for video
cards.front: On a 3-minute+ video ad, by when must you get a CTA in?
cards.back: Get at least a soft CTA in the first 60-90 seconds of video ads (~3 min+). This is part of Building Block #6 (CTA) - make the next action crystal clear because people are skimming.

cards.title: Ogilvy's reading chain
cards.front: What is the single job of every element from headline to CTA?
cards.back: Ogilvy's reading chain: each element exists to pull the reader into the next (headline -> subhead -> body -> CTA). The first line must compel reading all the way to the CTA.

cards.title: Copy length tiers (Mix It Up)
cards.front: What are the four copy-length tiers, and how many should you split-test?
cards.back:
MIX IT UP (Element 12) - split-test 3-4 versions:
- Ultra-short: 1-2 lines
- Short: ~3-5 lines (pairs with strong video; video sells)
- Long: 5+ lines (image ads selling a core product/consult; cold-traffic big asks)
- Long-ass: up to 1,000+ words (heavy persuasion for big cold-traffic asks)
Guidance: a solid selling video needs LESS copy; image ads selling a core product/consult call need LONGER copy.

cards.title: Long copy = qualified buyers
cards.front: Why would you deliberately PAY MORE per lead than your competitors?
cards.back: Long-copy and video ads produce higher-quality, more-qualified leads (the Keto ad was ~1,000 words). They pre-frame prospects for the next step. 'Cheap leads don't run businesses. Buyers run businesses.' Ideally pay MORE per lead than competitors - qualified leads convert.
cards.example: Keto long-copy ad (~1,000 words) pre-framed leads.

cards.title: UPSYD - Audience Awareness structures
cards.front: What does UPSYD stand for, and why does it change your copy depth?
cards.back:
UPSYD - the awareness ladder: Unaware -> Problem-aware -> Solution-aware -> Your-product-aware -> most-aware-Deal. Match copy depth and structure to where the prospect sits:
- Long-copy educational (Steps 2-3, Problem/Solution Aware): contrarian 'Big Aha' hook question -> debunk myths -> introduce solution -> further proof -> pivot to pitch.
- Long-copy problem-aware (Step 1-2): scary problem hook + study stat -> show results -> how/why you built solution -> scarcity offer -> clear CTA.
- Short-copy solution-aware (Steps 2-3): free-plus-shipping + scarcity + cost-reframe + social-cause framing + share request.

cards.title: Tell the story with a character, not 'you'
cards.front: When does 'you' scare prospects off - and what to use instead?
cards.back: Tell a story with a fictitious person, not 'you' - 'you' can feel like calling people out and scare them. Use a character; the reader still pictures themselves. Carousel ads work well for before/after storytelling.
cards.example: 'Jeff' in the Kabbage carousel downloads the app for a line of credit up to 100 grand.

cards.title: Like-ad copy rules
cards.front: What are the hard limits when writing a Like-ad?
cards.back:
Like-ad rules (Ch. 11):
- Body copy capped at 90 characters (track via wordcounter.net)
- Headlines can't be changed - body is the only editable text
- Check the mobile feed
- Honest CTA: 'Click Like to join'
- Don't make promises in exchange for the Like

cards.title: The 7 Power Questions
cards.front: Which Ryan Deiss framework do you run BEFORE finishing any copy - and what's its single goal?
cards.back:
The 7 Power Questions (Ryan Deiss, Ch. 20). Single goal: FOCUSED ATTENTION - if people don't focus, it won't convert. Answering even one or two well beats most competitors:
1. Make the offer NOVEL, UNIQUE, DISTINCTIVE?
2. Make it SIMPLE and EASY (grasp quickly -> like more, ascribe more validity)?
3. What OPENING QUESTION triggers a desire for consistency and drives action?
4. PRE-EXPOSE the audience to a concept linked to a desired emotional stimulus?
5. What MENTAL LINKS/ASSOCIATIONS (memory, nostalgia, identity) tie to the offer?
6. How can OPEN LOOPS hold attention and leverage the close (Zeigarnik effect)?
7. Create a visual/mental PORTAL the prospect passes through to new opportunities?

cards.title: Open loops & the Zeigarnik effect
cards.front: Why does closing your story too early switch off the reader's brain?
cards.back: OPEN LOOPS hold attention and leverage the close for cognitive closure (Zeigarnik effect - the 'waiter who forgot the order'). Weave the sales message mid-story and let story closure land AT the point of sale. Closing the story too early gives premature cognitive closure and switches off attention.

cards.title: Pre-publish copy check
cards.front: What seven boxes should you tick before publishing any ad copy?
cards.back:
Pre-publish copy check (7 Power Questions, Ch. 20):
- Does the offer appear novel, unique, distinctive?
- Is the offer simple and easy to understand?
- Is there an opening question that triggers consistency/action?
- Have I pre-exposed the audience to a desired emotional concept?
- Have I tapped mental links/associations (memory, nostalgia, identity)?
- Have I opened a loop to hold attention until the close?
- Have I created a mental/visual portal to new opportunity?

cards.title: Testimonial-gathering script (5 questions)
cards.front: What 5 questions extract a testimonial that overcomes objections and shows transformation?
cards.back:
Testimonial-gathering script (Ch. 15):
- What problem/pain/frustration were they solving before your product?
- What were they worried/hesitant about before buying? (overcomes objections)
- What were their results? How is life/business better now? (transformation)
- What did they like best about [your product]?
- Would they recommend it to a friend? If so, how can it help them?

cards.title: Fix a low Relevance Score
cards.front: Relevance Score is low - what three message-to-market fixes do you apply?
cards.back:
Fix a low Relevance Score = fix message-to-market match:
1. Confirm the offer is what people actually want
2. Rewrite copy to hit their biggest pains, desires, and fears
3. Move the prospect from the undesirable BEFORE state to the desirable AFTER state

cards.title: Don't make direct claims about the audience
cards.front: Why does 'Lose 30 lbs in 30 Days' get your ad banned - and what's the fix?
cards.back: Facebook bans direct claims/promises about the audience. 'Lose 30 lbs in 30 Days' gets banned - share your OWN results instead ('how I lost 28 lbs in six weeks'). Testimonials work, but some niches have rules (e.g. no before/after photos in weight loss).
cards.example: Banned: 'Lose 30 lbs in 30 Days' | OK: 'how I lost 28 lbs in six weeks'

cards.title: Never tie 'you' to a personal attribute
cards.front: Which seemingly harmless questions about 'you' violate Facebook Policy?
cards.back: Don't tie 'you' to a personal attribute - all of these violate Policy: 'Do you want to lose weight?', 'Do you have diabetes?', 'Do you want a thicker fuller beard?'. Compliant alternative: 'superfoods are some of the highest vibing food you will ever come across.'

cards.title: Avoid hyperbole and all-caps
cards.front: Two style habits that look scammy and repel customers?
cards.back:
- Don't exaggerate/use hyperbole: revealing words are overdone online; hyperbole repels more customers than it attracts. Avoid superlatives.
- Don't overdo all-caps: looks scammy and Facebook Policy notices; capitalize only the one key thing.

cards.title: Don't leave the next step unclear
cards.front: An ad got tons of social shares but no conversions - what was missing?
cards.back: Don't make the next step unclear. A high-share ad wasn't converting until the copy was revamped with a strong offer/CTA and a URL added to the copy - then it converted 'like crazy.' Always make the next step in the journey clear.
